title-group.article-title: The relationship between the gut microbiota and neuropsychological development and behaviour during childhood and adolescence: a systematic review of epidemiological studies

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Mounting evidence suggests that early-life microbial colonization might shape cognitive development and behaviour. This systematic review summarizes current research on the relationship between the gut microbiota and neuropsychological development and behaviour in children and adolescents (0–18 years). Following PRISMA guidelines, we conducted a comprehensive search across MEDLINE, Scopus, and Web of Science, identifying 78 eligible studies covering both clinical neurodevelopmental disorders and general-population neuropsychological outcomes.

We observed a high heterogeneity across studies regarding study design, statistical analyses and the consideration of confounding factors (e.g., diet, medication use), gut microbiota determinations and the assessment of neurobehavioural outcomes. Only 42% of studies used longitudinal designs, and confounding factors were frequently unaddressed. Microbiome alterations in autism spectrum disorder (n = 23 studies) included community structure shifts, elevated Clostridium and Sutterella, and reduced Blautia, Lactobacillus, and Bifidobacterium. Functional and metabolomic analyses suggest immunomodulatory and neuroactive processes as main contributors, including elevated levels of the short-chain fatty acids propionate and valerate. For attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (n = 7) findings were less consistent. Associations were modest in the general paediatric population, with Veillonella and Bifidobacterium appearing across multiple studies being more abundant in children with enhanced neuropsychological development. Overall, the evidence highlights potential microbial signatures associated with neurodevelopment, yet methodological limitations constrain causal inference. Most of the studies were of poor to fair quality, often due to technical shortcomings in microbiome assessment and statistical limitations.

Future research should prioritize standardized exposure/outcome assessment protocols and multi-omics integration, while underlining the potential of methodological rigour in translating findings into clinically actionable knowledge.

list-item.p: This systematic review synthesizes 78 studies linking gut microbiota to neuropsychological and behavioural development from birth to adolescence.
In ASD, alterations included elevated Clostridium and Sutterella, reduced Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, and Blautia, with implication of neuroactive and immunomodulatory pathways.
In ADHD, findings were less consistent; Roseburia, Agathobacter, Coprobacillus and Bifidobacterium emerged as microbial signals.
In the general paediatric population, Veillonella, Parabacteroides, Bifidobacterium, and Collinsella showed modest associations with neuropsychological development.
Substantial heterogeneity limited comparability; future studies should move towards harmonized protocols, longitudinal designs, and multi-omic approaches.

def.p: Developmental Origins of Health and Disease

Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
Strengthening The Organizing and Reporting of Microbiome Studies

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Brain development is an intricate succession of events that shape the neurobiological pathways underlying neurodevelopment (Chakraborty et al., 2021). Although it begins shortly after conception, the maturation of neural circuits remains incomplete for months after birth, with synaptic refinement and functional specialization continuing throughout childhood and adolescence (Holland et al., 1986). Thus, postnatal life is a critical neurodevelopmental window, during which tightly regulated processes lay the foundation for future complex skills (Huttenlocher and Dabholkar, 1997; Davison and Dobbing, 1966).

Coincident with this time period, gut microbiota (GM) establishment occurs during the first 36 months postpartum (Yatsunenko et al., 2012; Lynch and Pedersen, 2016). Briefly, newborns are initially and mostly colonised by Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium from the maternal microbiome (e.g., vaginal, breast). After weaning, dietary patterns influence the microbial composition, while microbial diversity increases steadily into adulthood (Rinninella et al., 2019). Simultaneously, the so-called gut microbiota-brain axis (GMBA) influences brain development through different communication lines, including neural (e.g., vagus nerve) and immune (e.g., microglia) pathways, but also by synthesising a myriad of metabolites that modulate brain physiology, such as neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin) and short chain fatty acids (SCFAs) (Muhammad et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022; Dalile et al., 2019). The latter are generated by fermentation of dietary fibres (Dalile et al., 2019) and have been shown to modulate the intestinal barrier integrity and the blood-brain barrier permeability, thereby affecting neural processes (Ahmed et al., 2022).

Notwithstanding, the vast majority of our knowledge on the interplay between the microbes in the gut and neurodevelopment arises from research in animal studies. In particular, when compared to their wild-type counterparts, GM depleted rodents have shown alterations in social behaviour (Desbonnet et al., 2015), motor activity (Heijtz et al., 2011), spatial, working and reference memory (Gareau et al., 2011). Interestingly, some of these effects can be reversed by subsequent exposure to specific microbial species, while others persisted despite late colonization (Clarke et al., 2013), underscoring the crucial role that the GMBA plays during the first periods of postnatal life. These facts dovetail with the Developmental Origins of Health and Disease (DOHaD) theory, which posits that early-life is key for programming disease risk later in life (Suzuki, 2018). Within this paradigm, the GM has arisen as a potential crucial factor for shaping lifelong neuropsychological outcomes (Sarkar et al., 2021).

To date, few studies are available focusing on infants and adolescents outside the clinical range. A growing body of evidence has reported associations between GM composition and neurodevelopmental disorders (NDD), which include autism spectrum disorder (ASD), intellectual disability, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and epilepsy, among others (Wang et al., 2022a; Korteniemi et al., 2023; Parenti et al., 2020). However, findings remain conflicting or inconclusive (Cryan et al., 2020), probably due to the high degree of variability across studies. Studying non-clinical populations is therefore critical to understanding microbial patterns associated with neurotypical development, while also enabling comparisons with clinical populations to determine whether microbial alterations observed in NDDs reflect pathological deviations or amplified patterns present in the general population.

Neuropsychological development is a major determinant of future life opportunities and mental health trajectories, with established associations with school dropout, unemployment, and lower health-related quality of life (Calvin et al., 2011; Deary et al., 2021; Oakley et al., 2021; Bosch et al., 2022; Erskine et al., 2016). Hence, delving into the link between GM and neuropsychological development offers promising insight into the mechanistic complexities of the GMBA, offering potential for interventions that could optimise neuropsychological trajectories and improve associated health outcomes.

Importantly, previous systematic reviews have primarily summarized reported associations without systematically assessing methodological rigor, particularly in ASD and ADHD populations. To address this gap, we apply the STORMS (Strengthening The Organizing and Reporting of Microbiome Studies) checklist to evaluate study quality and improve interpretability of findings (Mirzayi et al., 2021).

The aim of this systematic review is to comprehensively synthesize all the available literature on the relationship between the GM and neuropsychological development and behaviour in paediatric and adolescent populations, while providing guidance for future research in the field. Thus, our research question is: ‘What is the relationship between the gut microbiota and neuropsychological development and behaviour during childhood and adolescence?‘.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A protocol was written based on the methods set out in the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA) statement guidelines (Moher et al., 2009) [Moher et al., 2010], and registered on PROSPERO (Schiavo, 2019) (https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/prospero/), under the record No. CRD42023481460.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: The bibliographic search was conducted on November 21st, 2023 using MEDLINE (via PubMed, National Library of Medicine), Scopus (Elsevier) and Web of Science (WoS, via FECYT) search engines using combinations of keywords related to GM and neurodevelopment: (“gut microbiome” OR “gut microbiota” OR “gastrointestinal microbiome” OR “gut bacteria” OR “intestinal microbiome” OR “intestinal microbiota” OR “intestinal bacteria”) AND (“neurodevelopment” OR “neurodevelopmental disorders” OR “communication disorders” OR “intellectual disability” OR “learning disabilities” OR “motor skills disorders” OR “stereotypic movement disorder” OR “tic disorders” OR “autism” OR “ADHD” OR “hyperactivity” OR “cognition” OR “learning” OR “behaviour” OR “memory” OR “language”). Following establishment of search terms, Boolean operators were adjusted for each database. The detailed queries can be found in the Supplementary Table 1.

Initial screening of articles was performed by two independent researchers (PJ-A and MR-R) in Rayyan (Ouzzani et al., 2016), on the basis of the information provided in the title and abstract, in accordance with the predefined study eligibility criteria. Disagreements regarding inclusion or exclusion were addressed through discussion between the researchers, and with a third investigator (MG) providing a final decision when consensus could not be reached.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if: i) were of observational design (i.e., case-control, cross-sectional, longitudinal studies); ii) included GM measurements as an exposure (i.e., richness, diversity, relative abundance of particular taxa, biological activity); iii) used validated neuropsychological or behavioural measures or diagnoses of a NDD as an outcome; iv) included newborns, toddlers, children or adolescents (i.e., between the ages of 0 and 18) as study participants; and v) were published in English. Studies were excluded if meeting any of the following exclusion criteria: i) no full text availability; ii) not an original research piece (i.e., editorials, comments, reviews, systematic reviews, and unpublished studies); iii) involving other microbiota than intestinal; iv) not including quantitative GM measurements; or v) if it was an intervention study. In addition, studies examining the gut microbiome in relation to ADHD or ASD published prior to 2021 and 2022, respectively, were excluded a priori. This decision was based on the fact that two recent systematic reviews and meta-analyses had already comprehensively synthesized the literature on the gut microbiome in relation to ADHD and ASD up to these dates (Wang et al., 2022a; Korteniemi et al., 2023). The present review was therefore designed as an extension of the evidence base for ADHD and ASD, focusing on newly emerging studies for such diagnoses, broadening the scope beyond clinical NDD populations. By integrating general paediatric cohorts and dimensional neuropsychological outcomes, our review enables evaluation of whether microbial alterations observed in ADHD and ASD reflect disorder-specific pathophysiology or represent amplified patterns along the normative developmental spectrum. This approach, combined with a harmonized STORMS-based methodological quality appraisal, advances a developmentally informed, transdiagnostic perspective and maximizes interpretability without unnecessary duplication of prior work. Consequently, data from pre-2021/2022studies were not extracted or analysed, although their findings are briefly discussed in the discussion section of the present work.

sec.title: Data extraction and quality evaluation
sec.p: The data extracted from the publications included: first author and year of publication, country, sample size, age of the participants, outcome of interest and assessment instrument(s) used, GM taxonomical, functional, and GM-related biomarkers characterization methods, as well as statistical methods. Additionally, data on the results were collected, focusing on univariate summaries (e.g., alpha-diversity), community-wide analyses (e.g., beta-diversity, enterotyping), differential abundance analyses (DAA) at various taxonomic levels (phylum, class, order, family, genus, species), and functional and GM-related biomarkers.

On the basis of methods followed in previous systematic reviews (Gascon et al., 2015; Carvajal et al., 2025) (Gascon et al., 2015; Carvajal et al., 2025), and applying the domains stated by the STORMS guidelines for human microbiome research reporting (Mirzayi et al., 2021), a quality appraisal of the included publications was undertaken. Studies were scored based on each of the following domains: a) study design; b) participants; c) follow-ups; d) sample collection and shipment; e) sample storage; f) DNA extraction; g) host DNA depletion/microbial DNA enrichment; h) primer selection; i) positive controls; j) negative controls; k) contaminant mitigation and identification; l) replication; m) sequencing strategy; n) sequencing methods; o) blocking of batch effects; p) outcome data sources/measurement; q) handling of confounding; r) bioinformatic methods and sequence analysis; s) statistical analyses; t) treatment of missing data; u) subgroup analysis or/and sensitivity analysis; and v) provision of full analytical outputs. A detailed description of the quality assessment criteria is provided in Supplementary Table 2. The quality score for each study was calculated by summing the scores across all indicators and expressing them as a percentage of the maximum possible score. Based on the percentage, a quality degree was assigned to each manuscript: excellent (score ≥81%), good (between 61 and 80%), fair (between 41 and 60%), poor (between 21 and 40%) and very poor (≤20%) quality. Data extraction and quality evaluation of the studies was performed by PJ-A and subsequently verified by MF and MG.

Although meta-analysis represents best practice, substantial heterogeneity was identified across study designs, statistical approaches, and reported effect estimates. Given this multidimensional variability, we conducted a structured narrative synthesis instead of a meta-analysis. This approach allows for a transparent, methodologically appropriate summary of the evidence, avoiding potentially misleading pooled estimates while preserving critical nuances across studies.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
A total of 738 articles were identified in MEDLINE, 1713 in Scopus, and 1237 in WoS (Supplementary Table 1 and Fig. 1). Prior to screening, 1414 duplicate records were removed, along with 111 due to full-text unavailability, and 6 were excluded for not constituting original research. The remaining 2157 articles were uploaded to Rayyan for title and abstract screening (Ouzzani et al., 2016). From these, 154 articles were selected for full-text evaluation, of which 76 were finally excluded for one or more of the following reasons: i) ADHD or ASD studies published before 2021 or 2022, respectively, ii) not reporting associations between the GM and neuropsychological outcomes, iii) intervention studies, iv) not assessing child's GM, or v) including participants older than 18. Following the selection process, 78 studies were included in the present systematic review (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
PRISMA flow diagram of the article selection process.Fig. 1

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
The general characteristics and results of each included study are summarized in Table 1 and Fig. 2. Selected results are presented in Table 1, while the complete results are provided in Supplementary Table 3. Geographically, 30 of the studies were conducted in Asia —mainly in China (n = 21)— followed by Europe (n = 20), the USA (n = 16), and Africa (n = 4). Sample sizes ranged from 16 to 1784 participants (median = 80; mean = 151.2). Only 33 of the studies employed a longitudinal design. Notably, the design of the remaining studies (57.7%) limited causal inference, as the temporal sequence between the GM assessment and neuropsychological evaluation could not be firmly established. This was due to simultaneous collection of microbiota and neuropsychological data (i.e., cross-sectional design), pre-existing NDD diagnosis prior to GM sampling, or unclear reporting of timings. Based on the age categories defined by the American Medical Association, the faecal sample collection was most frequently performed in children (>one to 12 years, 74.3% of the studies), followed by infants (>one month to one year, 32.0%), neonates (birth to one month, 19.2%), and, to a lesser extent, adolescents (from 13 up to and including 18 years of age, 7.7%). Note that because many studies included participants from more than one age group, age categories are not mutually exclusive, and the percentages may exceed 100% (the total number of studies as the denominator). Similarly, neuropsychological assessments were primarily conducted in children (83.3% of the studies), with fewer studies focusing on infants (19.2%) and adolescents (8.9%), and a limited number evaluating outcomes in neonates (2.5%). Given the substantial variability in age groups, the specific ages at which GM and outcome measures were assessed are included in Table 1 and mentioned in the main text where relevant.Table 1
Characteristics and findings of the studies included in the systematic review.Table 1

Beta-diversity distances are taxonomical, unless otherwise stated. 6-GSI: Gastrointestinal Severity Index; ABC: Autism Behaviour Checklist; ADHD: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder; ADHD-RS: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Rating Scale; ADHD-SIB: Neurotypical Siblings of Individuals with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder; ADI-R: Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised; ADOS-1/2: Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, 1st/2nd Edition; ANOSIM: Analysis of Similarities; ASD-SIB: Neurotypical Siblings of Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder; ADQ: Autism Screening Questionnaire; ASQ-2/3: Ages and Stages Questionnaire, 2nd/3rd Edition; ASV: Amplicon Sequence Variant; BASC-2: Behaviour Assessment System for Children, 2nd Edition; BDI-2 ST: Batelle Developmental Inventory, 2nd Edition, Screening Test; BMI: Body Mass Index; BRIEF: Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function; BSID-II/III: Bayley Scales of Infant and Toddler Development, 2nd/3rd edition; CARS: Childhood Autism Rating Scale; CBCL: Child Behaviour Checklist; CBQ: Children's Behaviour Questionnaire; CPT3: Conners Continuous Performance Test, 3rd Edition; d: days; DAA: Differential Abundance Analysis; DMM: Dirichlet Multinomial Mixtures; DQ: Developmental Quotient; DSM-5: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition; ECBQ: Early Childhood Behaviour Questionnaire; ELISA: Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay; emPCR: Emulsion Polymerase Chain Reaction; epPCR: End Point Polymerase Chain Reaction; FSIQ: Full Scale IQ; GC(-MS): Gas Chromatography (-Mass Spectrometry); GDI: Gesell Development Inventory; GHBT: Glucose Hydrogen Breath Test; GLC: General Language Composite; GM: Gut Microbiota; GMDS-ER: Griffiths Mental Development Scales, Edition Revised; HPLC: High-Performance Liquid Chromatography; IBQ-R (SF): Infant Behaviour Questionnaire (Short Form); ICD-10: International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision; ITS: Internal Transcribed Spacer; K-ARS: Korean Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Rating Scale; KEGG: Kyoto Encyclopaedia of Genes and Genomes; ITS: Internal Transcribed Spacer; Lab-TAB: Laboratory Temperament Assessment Battery; LBP: Lipopolysaccharide-Binding Protein; LC-MS/MS: Liquid Chromatography-tandem Mass Spectrometry; LDA: Linear Discriminant Analysis; m: months; MAPI: Metagenomic Aerotolerant Predominance Index; M-CHAT: Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers; MARA: Mobile Autism Risk Assessment; MIO: Mixed-Integer Optimization; MLM: Multilevel Modelling; MRS: Man-Rogosa-Sharpe; MSEL: Mullen Scales of Early Learning; NNNS: NeoNatal Neurobehavioral Scale; ns: not statistically significant; n.sp.: not specified; LC/GM-MS: Liquid Chromatography/Gas Chromatography coupled to Mass Spectrometry; OTU: Operational Taxonomic Unit; PAM: Partitioning Around Medoids; PCR-DGGE: Polymerase Chain Reaction-Denaturing Gradient Gel Electrophoresis; PD: Phylogenetic Diversity; PERMANOVA: Permutational Analysis of Variance; PIQ: Performance IQ; PRC: Principal Response Curves; RDA: Redundancy Analysis; RDP: Ribosomal Database Project; (RT-)qPCR: (Real Time) Quantitative Polymerase Chain Reaction; SAS-A: Social Anxiety Scale for Adolescents; SB5: Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, Fifth Edition; sCD14: Soluble CD14; SCFA: Short Chain Fatty Acid; SCQ: Social Communication Questionnaire; SDQ: Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire; SNAP-IV: Swanson, Nolan and Pelham Teacher and Parent Rating Scale, 4th Edition; SRS(-2): Social Responsiveness Scale (2nd Edition); TOVA: Test of Variables of Attention; U(H)PLC-MS/MS: Ultrahigh Performance Liquid Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry; VFDB: Virulence Factor Database; VIQ: Verbal IQ; w: weeks; WCST: Wisconsin Card Sorting Test; WISC(-IV): Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (4th Edition); WPPSI-III/IV: Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence, 3rd/4th Edition; y: years; YGTSS: Yale Global Tic Severity Scale.Fig. 2
Characteristics of the included studies. A: methodological overview of included studies, including sample size, quality, address of confounders, study design, neuropsychological assessment methods, microbial profiling techniques, and inclusion of microbial-derived biomarkers. B: global distribution of studies by country. Countries without data are shown in white. NDD: neurodevelopmental disorder; GM: gut microbiota.Fig. 2

Microbial and metabolomic profiling techniques were relatively consistent across the included studies. Most studies employed 16 S rRNA gene sequencing (76.9%), while only 13 utilized shotgun metagenomics (16.6%), as can be observed in Fig. 2. Additional approaches included internal transcribed spacer sequencing for fungal profiling (6.4%) and quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) for targeted microbial quantification (3.8%). Metabolomic analyses were only included in 18 studies (23.1%), primarily leveraging liquid or gas chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry; a few studies also used immunological assays (e.g. ELISA) to quantify specific immune-related biomarkers linked to the GM (6.4%). By contrast, the pipelines used to process GM data significantly differed across studies. Tools for preprocessing, as well as software and reference databases used for taxonomic and functional profiling differed notably between publications (see Table 1). Moreover, reporting of these methodological details was often incomplete, with several studies omitting key information (Supplementary Tables 3 and 4).

Neuropsychological evaluation methods also varied considerably. Across all studies, outcome measurement instruments encompassed direct observational scales administered by trained professionals in 48 studies (61.5%), parent- or teacher-reported measures in 27 of the papers (34.6%), and self-reported measures (3.8%), primarily in articles evaluating older children.

General population-based studies evaluated a broad range of developmental, neuropsychological, and behavioural outcomes in the general paediatric population. These included assessments of behaviour and emotional responses (e.g., Child Behaviour Checklist, 11.3% of the studies), temperament (e.g., Infant Behaviour Questionnaire, 10.3%), social functioning (e.g., Social Communication Questionnaire, 4.1%), neuropsychological functions (e.g., Behaviour Rating Inventory of Executive Functioning, 10.3%), and different core dimensions of early neurodevelopment (e.g., Ages and Stages Questionnaire, 21.6%). Conversely, diagnosis-based studies used formal clinical diagnoses based on Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) or International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (ICD) criteria (27.9%), often supported by validated scales such as the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition. Beyond diagnostic classification, NDD severity (e.g., Childhood Autism Rating Scale, 5.2%) and NDD-signs (e.g., Autism Behaviour Checklist, 9.3%) were measured using caregiver reports on validated instruments.

sec.title: Study quality assessment
sec.p: The quality score of the studies ranged from 6.8 to 87.5%, with 70.5% of the studies classified as of “very poor”, “poor” or “fair” quality. Lower ratings were often received due to the absence of negative and positive controls (i.e., mock communities) during sequencing, lack of biological and technical replicates, and failure to apply randomization strategies to mitigate batch effects. Additionally, several studies inadequately addressed confounding variables and failed to implement imputation methods for missing data. The results of the quality assessment for each included study are presented in Supplementary Table 4. While this systematic review includes and reports findings from all studies, greater emphasis is placed on those classified as “good” or “excellent” quality.

sec.title: Study findings
sec.p: The present systematic review reports findings separately for diagnosis- and sign-based studies, on one hand, and those based on the general paediatric population, on the other hand. To facilitate interpretation and consistency, the results of diagnosis- and sign-based studies are presented by diagnostic codes. In contrast, findings from general population studies were grouped by neuropsychological or behavioural outcome based on the classification provided in the original articles and/or in the psychometric validation papers cited in the original studies. Findings described below may be found in either Table 1 and Supplementary Table 3, as specified.

sec.title: Diagnosis- and sign-based studies
sec.p: Thirty-one studies investigated the association between the GM and NDDs, including ADHD, autism, and tic disorders diagnosis and signs (Alookaran et al., 2022; Bojović et al., 2020; Galova et al., 2022a, 2022b; He et al., 2023; Jones et al., 2022; Jung et al., 2022; Kakabadze et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2022; Loughman et al., 2020; Mendive Dubourdieu and Guerendiain, 2023; Qu et al., 2023; Tataru et al., 2023; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Yitik Tonkaz et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b, 2023a, 2023b; Wang and Fu, 2023; Xi et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2022a; Xu et al., 2023; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a, 2023b, 2023c; Deng et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023) (Table 1). One study measured the occurrence of any NDD as defined by the ICD-10 and reported lower richness in the case group (Bojović et al., 2020). Similarly, another study jointly comparing ADHD and ASD subjects with the neurotypical population found lower richness and Faith's phylogenetic diversity (PD) in those diagnosed (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023).

Focusing on ADHD, the six (Jung et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2022; Loughman et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022b, 2023b; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023) studies included in this systematic review had quality scores ranging from 28.6% to 67.9%. Four (Lee et al., 2022; Loughman et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022b, 2023b) out of the six (Jung et al., 2022; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023) articles included in this systematic review found no associations between microbial richness or diversity and ADHD. These studies measured faecal samples collected across a broad age range, from one month to 18 years. In contrast, Cassidy-Bushrow et al. observed significantly higher Faith's PD at six months of age in ADHD participants as compared to their neurotypical counterparts (Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023), whereas Jung et al. reported opposite findings (Jung et al., 2022). Regarding beta-diversity, four of the five studies assessing microbial community structure reported significant differences between children with ADHD and their neurotypical counterparts. Several studies also assessed differences in the relative abundance of specific bacterial taxa between children with ADHD and their neurotypical peers. For example, Roseburia and Agathobacter (Lee et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023b) were consistently found to have direct associations with ADHD. In contrast, Prevotella (Loughman et al., 2020; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022c), Bifidobacterium (Jung et al., 2022; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023), Coprobacillus (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023), and Lactobacillus (Jung et al., 2022; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023) were repeatedly reported to be inversely associated with ADHD. However, some genera demonstrated inconsistent associations across studies (e.g. Ruminococcus (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023)), and others varied in directionality within the same study depending on the sampling time point (i.e. Streptococcus, Bacteroides (Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023)). Only two of the six studies assessed gut microbial functional profiles or GM-derived metabolites in relation to ADHD, but no consistent findings across studies were identified for the metabolites assessed (Jung et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b).

Regarding ASD, 22 studies assessed clinical diagnosis, symptom severity, related behaviour, or early signs (Alookaran et al., 2022; Galova et al., 2022a, 2022b; He et al., 2023; Jones et al., 2022; Kakabadze et al., 2022; Mendive Dubourdieu and Guerendiain, 2023; Qu et al., 2023; Tataru et al., 2023; Yitik Tonkaz et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a; Wang and Fu, 2023; Xie et al., 2022a; Xu et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a, 2023b; Deng et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023). Overall, study quality ranged from 6.9% to 82.1%, and most studies reported no statistically significant differences in GM richness or diversity between children with ASD and neurotypical controls. Nevertheless, five studies observed reduced richness in ASD participants (He et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023c), whereas two found the opposite (Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a). Only one study identified higher diversity indices in children with ASD (Fujishiro et al., 2023), whereas three found greater diversity in neurotypical peers (Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023a). Similarly, in children with tic disorders, no significant differences were observed in richness or diversity indices (Xi et al., 2021). These results are derived from faecal samples collected from participants aged between 2 and 15 years, reflecting considerable age-related heterogeneity across studies. Several studies also performed community-wide analyses (Alookaran et al., 2022; He et al., 2023; Jones et al., 2022; Qu et al., 2023; Tataru et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a; Wang and Fu, 2023; Xie et al., 2022a; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023). As summarized in Table 1 and Supplementary Table 3, significant differences in community composition were found in eight (He et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a; Xie et al., 2022a; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023) out of the thirteen studies that evaluated beta-diversity between ASD children and controls. In turn, multiple taxa were found to be associated with ASD in more than one study. At the genera level, Clostridium (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Wan et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023), Sutterella (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023), Megamonas (Deng et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019), and Prevotella (He et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2023) were found to be more abundant in ASD cases. Conversely, Bifidobacterium (Yitik Tonkaz et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022), Blautia (Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2022a; Deng et al., 2022), Lactobacillus (Galova et al., 2022a; Liu et al., 2019), and Eggerthella (Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Chamtouri et al., 2023c) were inversely associated with increased ASD signs or ASD diagnosis. Notably, both Streptococcus and Bacteroides showed conflicting directions across studies (Galova et al., 2022a, 2022b; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a). Regarding the functional profiling and GM-derived metabolites, elevated levels of propionic (He et al., 2023; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022) and valeric acid (Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022) during infancy and childhood were reported to be linked to ASD diagnosis and severity in some studies. Importantly, no studies reported associations of these SCFAs with ASD in the opposite direction. Additionally, glutamate (Vernocchi et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a), glycine (Wan et al., 2022; Chamtouri et al., 2023a), methionine (Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a), tryptophan (Wan et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a), and lysine levels or its related pathways (Vernocchi et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023a; Chamtouri et al., 2023a) were also suggested to be implicated in biological underpinnings of ASD.

Only one study addressed tic disorders (Xi et al., 2021), with a study quality score of 64.3%. While no significant differences in alpha-diversity were observed between cases and controls, the study reported statistically significant differences in beta-diversity. DAA revealed increased abundance of Bacteroides plebeius and Ruminococcus lactaris in cases, as well as at the differences at the functional level (e.g., reduced activity of GABA degradation pathways).

sec.title: Neuropsychological and behavioural outcomes in the general paediatric population
Behavioural, emotional, and adaptive functioning problems
sec.p: Eleven studies examined associations between the gut microbiota and behavioural-emotional problems, with quality scores ranging from 42.9% to 71.4%. Seven of these studies evaluated alpha-diversity indices (Lee et al., 2022; Kraaij et al., 2023; Flannery et al., 2020; Laue et al., 2022a; Ou et al., 2022, 2023a, 2023b; van de Wouw et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021; Nieto-Ruiz et al., 2023). For richness, only two reported statistically significant associations: Wei et al. found higher bacterial observed taxa in children with lower prosocial behaviour (Wei et al., 2022), while Laue et al. reported that greater richness at six weeks of life was associated with higher depression signs at three years of age and that greater richness at two years of age was linked to lower adaptive skills in boys and increased attentional problems in girls, both assessed at age three (Laue et al., 2022a). Of the seven studies assessing alpha-diversity and behavioural/emotional problems (Kraaij et al., 2023; Laue et al., 2022a; Ou et al., 2022, 2023a, 2023b; van de Wouw et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022), three reported significant associations (Laue et al., 2022a; van de Wouw et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Notably, both van de Wouw et al. and Laue et al. identified higher bacterial diversity indices in children with lower internalizing problems (Laue et al., 2022a; van de Wouw et al., 2022). Other findings were dimension-, age- and sex-dependent, and not consistently replicated across studies. For instance, although both Laue et al. and Wei et al. reported significant associations between bacterial diversity and social behaviour, the direction of the associations was opposite (Laue et al., 2022a; Wei et al., 2022).

Some associations between specific genera and behavioural and emotional outcomes were reported in more than one study. For example, higher relative abundance of Veillonella was associated with aggressive behaviour, anxiety/depression signs, emotional reactivity, and externalizing behaviour in two studies (Flannery et al., 2020; van de Wouw et al., 2022). Flannery et al. and van de Wouw et al. reported Collinsella to be more abundant in children with internalizing behaviour and anxiety/depression signs, emotional reactivity, and somatic complaints (Flannery et al., 2020; van de Wouw et al., 2022). Additionally, two studies found higher Bifidobacterium in children with better adaptive skills and fewer internalizing behaviours (Ou et al., 2022; Laue et al., 2022b). In contrast, a third study associated increases Bifidobacteria with greater aggressive and anxiety/depression signs (Christian et al., 2015). As it can be observed in Table 1, other genera (e.g., Eggethella, Blautia, Alistipes) exhibited non-replicable or directionally inconsistent associations across studies.

sec.title: Temperament
sec.p: Ten studies evaluated temperament as an outcome (Flannery et al., 2020; Christian et al., 2015; Aatsinki et al., 2019a; Delgadillo et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2023; Fox et al., 2021; Huovinen et al., 2023; Kelsey et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2022b), with study quality ranging between 39.3% and 75.0%. Only four studies found statistically significant results with beta-diversity, predominantly only in boys (Christian et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2023; Fox et al., 2021; Huovinen et al., 2023). Regarding alpha-diversity, the findings were sex- and timepoint-dependent, and not replicated across datasets (Christian et al., 2015; Delgadillo et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2023; Huovinen et al., 2023; Kelsey et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2022b; Aatsinki et al., 2019b; Fox et al., 2022). For example, Christian et al. linked PD whole tree and Shannon index to children scoring higher in surgency/extraversion and sociability, in boys with elevated high-intensity pleasure, and in girls with lower effortful control (Christian et al., 2015). In contrast, Fan et al. found lower Shannon indices in neonates with lower punctuations in sadness, in infants with lower impulsivity, and in children with lower high-intensity pleasure, cuddliness, and effortful control (Fan et al., 2023).

With respect to DAAs, the most frequently reported genera associated with temperament outcomes across studies were Bifidobacterium, Akkermansia, and Parabacteroides. Bifidobacterium was identified in three studies, being positively linked with surgency/extraversion, perceptual sensitivity, soothability, and regulation/orienting (Fox et al., 2021; Kelsey et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Akkermansia was found to be more abundant in children with elevated scores in effortful control, cuddliness, sadness, fear, and surgency/extraversion (Delgadillo et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). Finally, Parabacteroides was found to be related to increased sociability (Christian et al., 2015), fear (Flannery et al., 2020), and negative reactivity (Huovinen et al., 2023). However, these associations were sex-dependent, not consistently observed in the same temperamental domains across studies, and not all in the same direction (Flannery et al., 2020; Christian et al., 2015; Aatsinki et al., 2019a; Delgadillo et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2023; Fox et al., 2021; Huovinen et al., 2023; Kelsey et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2022b).

sec.title: Social competence
sec.p: Out of the four studies assessing social behavioural outcomes (Deng et al., 2022; Ou et al., 2023a; Kim et al., 2022; Laue et al., 2020), only one found statistically significant associations, with increased alpha-diversity in infants with higher self-reported social anxiety (Deng et al., 2022). Regarding GM community structure, Laue et al. found links between beta-diversity at 1 year of age and social responsiveness later in childhood (Laue et al., 2020). Additionally, Ou et al. reported gut bacterial clusters derived using Dirichlet Multinomial Mixtures to be associated with self-reported social anxiety symptoms (Ou et al., 2023a). DAAs across studies identified few consistent taxa associated with social outcomes (Deng et al., 2022; Ou et al., 2023a; Kim et al., 2022; Laue et al., 2020), but as evidenced in Table 1 and Supplementary Table 3, results were heterogeneous, with unexisting overlap in specific taxa. The study quality score for the studies focused on social behavioural outcomes ranged between 46.4% and 60.7%.

sec.title: Neuropsychological functions
sec.p: Across the nine studies assessing neuropsychological functions, study quality scores ranged from 42.9% to 87.5%. Of these, measures of richness and diversity were predominantly not statistically significantly associated with the outcomes (Eckermann et al., 2022; Fujihara et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023a; van de Wouw et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2023). Notably, Lapidot et al. found a direct relationship between Pielou's evenness and Shannon's diversity and the full scale intelligence quotient (FSIQ) (Lapidot et al., 2023). In the opposite direction, Streit et al. reported an inverse association between Shannon's index and intellectual functioning (Streit et al., 2021). Community-level analyses yielded non-statistically significant associations, with only Lapidot et al. describing a link between beta-diversity (i.e., Bray-Curtis, UniFrac, Jensen-Shannon) and FSIQ as measured by the Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, Fifth Edition. At the taxonomic resolution, no genera or species demonstrated cross-study replicability or directional concordance in their associations with cognitive outcomes (Fujihara et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023a; Zhou et al., 2023; Lapidot et al., 2023; Streit et al., 2021). Similarly, analyses of functional pathways and GM-derived metabolites also revealed heterogeneous results (see Supplementary Table 3).

Two studies investigated GM features in relation to attentional outcomes, and neither of them identified statistically significant associations with alpha-diversity indices (Wang et al., 2022b; Aatsinki et al., 2022). The Finnish study, conducted in infants, found no statistically significant differences in beta-diversity based on Bray-Curtis dissimilarity. However, taxonomic DAA revealed higher Clostridium and lower Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Prevotella and Haemophilus in children with increased fear bias (Aatsinki et al., 2022).

sec.title: Early neurodevelopment
sec.p: Twenty studies conducted early neurodevelopmental assessments using standardized evaluations (Wei et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021, 2023; Kort et al., 2021; Matsuyama et al., 2022; Rothenberg et al., 2021; Roze et al., 2020; Sarkar et al., 2022; Sordillo et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020, 2023b; Tamana et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021; Carlson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022; Cho et al., 2023; Donowitz et al., 2022a; Guo et al., 2023; Guzzardi et al., 2022; Hunter et al., 2023), with quality scores ranging from 6.9% to 75.0%. Of these, Chen et al., Guo et al., and Carlson et al. reported inverse relationships between gut bacterial richness and language development (Carlson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2023). In contrast, Sarkar et al. found that neonatal bacterial richness was directly correlated with future adaptive and personal-social development (Sarkar et al., 2022), while Carlson et al. observed that infants with lower richness scored lower in future learning and visual perception (Carlson et al., 2018). Regarding microbial diversity, two studies found inverse relationships with cognitive development, language and visual perception (Carlson et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2023), whereas one reported correlations in the opposite direction (Sarkar et al., 2022).

The relationship between gut microbial community structure and neurodevelopment was less frequently examined. However, ten of the studies reported associations between diverse distance metrics (Chen et al., 2022) or between enterotypes (Rothenberg et al., 2021; Roze et al., 2020; Sarkar et al., 2022; Sordillo et al., 2019; Tamana et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021; Carlson et al., 2018) and early neurodevelopment.

Taxonomic DAA revealed several taxa-specific associations. Veillonella was linked to improved motor and cognitive development (Acuña et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2023; Guzzardi et al., 2022). Additionally, two studies found lower Parabacteroides relative abundance in children scoring higher in language and fine motor skills (Kort et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021). According to three publications, the Bifidobacterium genus was found to be more abundant in infants and children with superior practical reasoning, cognitive development, motor, and language development (Kort et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021; Guzzardi et al., 2022). At the species level, B. catenulatum, B. adolescentis, and B. bifidum were related to improved language and motor development (Kort et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021). In contrast, two studies found inverse associations between this genus and early neurodevelopmental outcomes (Cho et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2023). Functional and metabolic findings were limited and inconclusive (Kort et al., 2021; Donowitz et al., 2022b), as detailed in Supplementary Table 3.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review synthesizes scientific evidence on the relationship between the GM and neuropsychological development and behaviour from the neonatal period up to 18 years of age. Drawing on 78 eligible studies, the findings suggest a number of potential patterns amid substantial heterogeneity and emphasise the need for greater methodological harmonisation to advance the field.

sec.title: Diagnosis- and sign-based studies
sec.p: Most of the diagnosis- and sign-based studies reported no consistent differences in alpha-diversity between subjects with NDDs (basically ADHD and ASD) and their neurotypical pairs. Nevertheless, a subset of studies reported reduced richness and diversity in ASD at older ages (He et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Zhao et al., 2023b). This age-specific pattern may reflect greater phenotypic differentiation later in development, which could make associations with the GM more detectable. Notably, the vast majority of studies included found differences in microbial community structure between NDD cases and neurotypical pairs, an observation consistent with the conclusions of two previous systematic reviews both including more than 100 studies published prior to 2021 for ADHD (Wang et al., 2022a) and 2022 for ASD (Korteniemi et al., 2023). However, the predominance of cross-sectional studies limits causal inference and precludes ruling out the possibility of reverse causation.

In line with the most recent meta-analysis on GM in ADHD —which initially identified a higher Shannon index in ADHD that lost statistical significance after accounting for the high heterogeneity across studies (Wang et al., 2022a)— our findings indicate that alpha-diversity metrics rarely show significant differences between cases and controls. Similarly, a prior systematic review of GM in ASD reported inconsistent findings on alpha-diversity: half of the studies found no significant differences, while the remaining were divided between higher and lower diversity in ASD compared to neurotypicals (Korteniemi et al., 2023). Taken together, the lack of consensus challenges the validity of generalized diversity-condition paradigms in neurodevelopmental research.

In our systematic review, taxon-level analyses yielded few consistently replicated associations: Roseburia and Agathobacter were often enriched in ADHD (Lee et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023b), whereas Prevotella, Bifidobacterium, Coprobacillus, and Lactobacillus were often depleted (Jung et al., 2022; Loughman et al., 2020; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022b; Cassidy-Bushrow et al., 2023). In ASD, elevated relative abundances of Clostridium, Sutterella, Megamonas, and Prevotella (He et al., 2023; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023; Deng et al., 2022; Fujishiro et al., 2023; Wan et al., 2020), and statistically significant reductions in Bifidobacterium, Blautia, Lactobacillus, and Eggerthella were noted (He et al., 2023; Bundgaard-Nielsen et al., 2023; Yitik Tonkaz et al., 2023; Vernocchi et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2022a; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022; Galova et al., 2022b). Importantly, several of these genera demonstrate biological plausibility. For instance, Lactobacillus spp. raise mucosal IL-10 and upregulate host GABA and PPAR-γ signalling (Jhun et al., 2021), thus having anti-inflammatory properties. In contrast, Sutterella has been shown to degrade IgA and increase IL-8, potentially promoting local inflammation (Zhou et al., 2025). Similarly, Eggethella has been shown to induce proinflammatory shifts by raising IL-17 and IFN-γ levels, while depleting host namide adenine dinucleotide and altering bile-acid and succinylcarnitine metabolism (Balakrishnan et al., 2023). Together, these findings indicate that the ADHD/ASD-associated genera may modulate the GMBA via immune effects (Nie et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021) as one of the plausible pathways.

Taxonomic overlaps were found between our systematic review and the meta-analysis by Wang et al., particularly the inverse associations between Prevotella and Bifidobacterium relative abundances and ADHD (Wang et al., 2022a). Regarding ASD, the last systematic review in the field reported lower Bifidobacterium and enriched Clostridium and Sutterella in ASD (Korteniemi et al., 2023; Lewandowska-Pietruszka et al., 2023), aligning with our findings. Nonetheless, several studies have described associations in the opposite direction (Korteniemi et al., 2023). A large body of research supports the influence of breastfeeding and dietary patterns on GM (Ma et al., 2020; De Filippo et al., 2010; Bokulich et al., 2016). Given that children with NDDs are more likely to exhibit food selectivity (Bandini et al., 2010), it is plausible that some of the divergent results observed across studies are attributable to residual confounding. For example, Yap et al. suggested that the association between GM was not causal but indeed attributable to the mediating effect of ASD-related dietary preferences (Yap et al., 2021). Therefore, adjusting analyses for potential confounders is essential to avoid spurious associations.

Several microbial-derived metabolites and functional pathways were found to be potentially relevant to NDDs. Notably, elevated levels of propionic and valeric acid in feces early in life were associated with ASD across some studies (He et al., 2023; Chamtouri et al., 2023c; Deng et al., 2022), which is in line with previous evidence reporting SCFAs overexpression in ASD (Wang et al., 2012; Pulikkan et al., 2018). SCFAs are neuroactive bacterial fermentation byproducts that have been shown to play a role in neuro-immuno-endocrine regulation (Dalile et al., 2019; Silva et al., 2020), blood-brain barrier permeability (Fock and Parnova, 2023), or neurotransmitter synthesis (Nankova et al., 2014; Clarke et al., 2014). Our findings reinforce the putative role of these microbial-derived luminal metabolites in ASD-related traits, consistent with the DOHaD framework, given their relevance during early-life windows (Suzuki, 2018). However, the relationship between SCFAs and NDDs deserves further investigation through longitudinal or interventional studies.

sec.title: General paediatric population
sec.p: In our systematic review, current findings linking the GM to attentional and social functioning in the general paediatric population remained limited, largely due to the paucity of studies published to date. Associations with neuropsychological outcomes were also modest, despite a relatively larger number of studies. By contrast, research focusing on behavioural and emotional traits repeatedly revealed Veillonella, Collinsella, and Bifidobacterium as microbial correlates, albeit not consistently within the same domains (Flannery et al., 2020; Laue et al., 2022a; Ou et al., 2022, 2023a, 2023b; van de Wouw et al., 2022). With regard to early neurodevelopment, decreased microbial richness was linked to improved language development in a few studies (Carlson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2023), while lower diversity associated with better visual perception and cognitive and communicative development (Carlson et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2023). However, alpha-diversity indices may oversimplify the intricacies within the microbial community as they are context-dependent and sensitive to technical biases (Cassol et al., 2025; Willis, 2019). Additionally, abundances of Veillonella, Parabacteroides, and different Bifidobacterium species were related to motor, communicative and cognitive development, including superior practical reasoning functioning (Kort et al., 2021; Acuña et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2023; Guzzardi et al., 2022).

Several of the repeatedly implicated genera have plausible neurobiological effects. For example, Bifidobacteria have been reported to ferment sugars to acetate and lactate, fuelling other SCFA-producers, and uniquely synthesize neuroactive compounds such as GABA (Pokusaeva et al., 2017). They also enhance mucosal immunity by increasing secretory IgA, IL-10, and regulatory T cells (Chen et al., 2021), thereby promoting gut-immune homeostasis. Similarly, Veillonella spp. ferment lactate into acetate and propionate, which are known to support colonic epithelial health and systemic immune homeostasis (Zhang and Huang, 2023). Paradoxically, V. parvula have been suggested to produce a highly active lipopolysaccharide that potently engages TLR4, driving macrophage IL-6, IL-1beta and TNF-alpha release and promoting colonic inflammation (Zhan et al., 2022).

sec.title: Future directions
sec.p: The increasing number of recently published studies highlights that the role of the gut microbiota in brain development is a rapidly emerging field. However, the current evidence does not yet support the existence of a consistently replicated microbial profile or characteristics associated with neuropsychological trajectories. The substantial degree of heterogeneity —primarily in terms of study design, statistical approaches, and exposure and outcome assessment— has not only hampered the identification of consistent and conclusive results but also precluded the possibility of conducting a meta-analysis (Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews, 2019). In particular, the absence of commensurable effect estimates, together with variability in both exposure and outcome assessment and timing, would likely yield pooled estimates driven by methodological artefacts rather than true biological associations.

To advance the field and generate robust, interpretable evidence on the relationship between the GM and neuropsychological outcomes, future studies should address several methodological limitations observed in the current literature. While cross-sectional studies remain useful for hypothesis generation, they do not allow the establishment of a causal relationship. Since neurobiological triggers of development have early origins, but may not fully manifest until later in life, it remains unclear whether the GM plays a causal role, modulates expression, or reflects underlying genetic factors. In this regard, only 42.3% (33/78) of the included studies adopted a longitudinal design. Thus, future research should prioritize longitudinal methodologies and explore gene-microbiota interactions to disentangle temporal relationships and reduce the likelihood of reverse causation.

Importantly, numerous exposures influence both GM composition and neurodevelopmental processes, including delivery type (Reyman et al., 2019), diet (Ho et al., 2018; David et al., 2014), gastrointestinal infections (Chung The and Le, 2022), and medication use (Boonchooduang et al., 2025; Jernberg et al., 2007). However, these variables were often unmeasured or inadequately addressed across studies. For instance, medication use in ADHD may confound microbiome-outcome associations (Chan et al., 2023), yet few studies implemented sensitivity analyses (e.g., excluding children with GI diseases or controlling for psychiatric medication). Thus, residual confounding and selection bias remain major threats to validity. To strengthen causal inference, future research should incorporate not only rigorous control of confounding, but also other advanced epidemiological approaches, including multiple imputation of missing data, inverse probability weighting, and mendelian randomization, when applicable.

In addition, standardized protocols for microbial assessment are critically needed. Despite the availability of guidelines such as STORMS (Mirzayi et al., 2021), many studies failed to report key methodological parameters, including sampling timing, use of DNA stabilizers, storage conditions, DNA extraction protocols, and contamination controls (see Supplementary Table 4). Profiling methods also varied widely; for instance, culture-based methods and qPCR are prone to quantification and amplification biases. Consistent application of best practices in sample handling, sequencing, and data preprocessing is key for reproducibility and comparability (Sinha et al., 2017; Vandeputte et al., 2017). Furthermore, studies should adopt high-taxonomic resolution approaches to move beyond genus-level associations. In the same direction, outcome assessment must also be harmonized. For clinical diagnoses of NDD, standardized diagnostic criteria (e.g., DSM, ICD) should be favoured over caregiver reports, while in population-based studies, the use of shared conceptual frameworks and validated instruments would facilitate cross-study comparisons (Júlvez et al., 2016; Bondi et al., 2024).

Future work should also integrate multi-omic approaches. Only a minority of studies in this review incorporated functional (19.2%) or metabolic profiling (39.7%). Including metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, metaproteomics, and metabolomics from different biological matrices can offer mechanistic insights into microbiota-brain interactions (Valles-Colomer et al., 2016; Menni et al., 2017). As emphasized by Shanahan and Hill (2019), risk-factor analyses without consideration of the underlying biological mechanisms may produce findings with limited interpretability and utility. Thus, integrating different omics layers is key to uncovering GMBA mechanisms (Shimonovich et al., 2021).

Additionally, statistical methods must reflect the compositional and sparse nature of microbiome data. Despite continuing efforts, no consensus exists on best practices for DAA, with many studies applying inconsistent or suboptimal approaches. For example, rarefaction is commonly used to standardize sequencing depth, but it has been criticized for reducing power and inflating false positives when used with standard tests (Weiss et al., 2017; McMurdie and Holmes, 2014). Choice of statistical model is also critical, as methods differ in their ability to control false discovery rates and address compositionality, leading to inconsistent results (Hawinkel et al., 2019; Nearing et al., 2022; Aitchison et al., 2025; Morton et al., 2019).

Finally, harmonisation of key methodological components may enable cross-cohort analyses and significantly enhance statistical power of existing studies (Agronah and Bolker, 2025). As demonstrated by international consortia like Pregnancy And Childhood Epigenetics (Felix et al., 2018), large-scale collaborations can leverage meta-analytic frameworks to aggregate results across multiple cohorts, thereby increasing reproducibility and generalizability (Júlvez et al., 2016). However, the choice of strategy often depends on the omic layer and the feasibility of phenotype harmonisation. Genomic and epigenomic consortia such as Early Growth Genetics and EArly Genetics and Lifecourse Epidemiology (Middeldorp et al., 2019) have applied discovery-replication designs when cognitive or developmental phenotypes are difficult to harmonize. While pooled analyses are less common in genomics due to data-sharing constraints, initiatives like the Global Neurodegeneration Proteomics Consortium illustrate their potential, having successfully pooled proteomic data across multiple cohorts (Bringmann et al., 2024). Altogether, hybrid frameworks that integrate harmonized data across cohorts represent a promising avenue for advancing the study of neurodevelopment and its biological underpinnings.

To support this progress, and given that this research area lies at the intersection of several distinct fields (i.e., microbiology, bioinformatics, neuropsychology, epidemiology), the formation of interdisciplinary teams is essential to ensure methodological rigor and conceptual soundness throughout all phases of research.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review of 78 studies identified a number potential patterns in gut microbial composition and highlighted specific taxonomic and functional links with neuropsychological and behavioural outcomes from birth to adolescence.

While reductions in alpha-diversity were inconsistently reported in ASD studies, shifts in microbial community structure and taxa were repeatedly observed, including Clostridium, Sutterella, Megamonas, Prevotella, Bifidobacterium, Blautia, Lactobacillus, and Eggerthella. The plausibility of these results was supported by functional and metabolomic analyses, which involved immunomodulatory and neuroactive pathways with particular importance of SCFAs. Findings in ADHD were less consistent but pointed alterations in Roseburia, Agathobacter, Prevotella, Bifidobacterium, Coprobacillus, and Lactobacillus populations as potential microbial signatures. Finally, in the general paediatric population, microbial associations were modest, though genera such as Veillonella, Parabacteroides, Bifidobacterium, and Collinsella emerged as candidates linked to neuropsychological development.

Notwithstanding, heterogeneity in study designs, exposure/outcome measurements, and analysis methods prevents firm conclusions or meta-analyses. Future research would benefit from interdisciplinary collaboration and well-powered longitudinal cohorts, harmonized protocols, GM data at the strain-level resolution and integrated multi-omics approaches. Coupled with robust epidemiological methods, these efforts are critical to elucidating microbial influences on neuropsychological development and informing future microbiome-targeted interventions.
